title: Russia Grain Export Ban → Wheat Spike
summary: Russia banned grain exports following a severe drought and wildfires that damaged its 2010 harvest, reducing expected global exportable wheat supply sharply.
mechanism: Russian drought and wildfire damage triggered an export ban, removing a major Black Sea exporter from the market and forcing importers to seek alternative supply at higher prices.
causal_chain: drought and fires damage Russian grain output
Russia bans grain exports
global wheat importers must source elsewhere
wheat prices reprice higher on tighter export supply

forecast.note: Bullish wheat; magnitude depends on duration of ban and ability of other exporters to fill gap

outcome.realized_note: Sharp late-summer wheat spike in August 2010. MacroTrends wheat chart shows 2010 annual high at $6.9425.
outcome.chart_note: MacroTrends wheat historical chart shows sharp late-summer 2010 rise
outcome.reversal_note: Prices moderated as other suppliers (U.S., Australia, EU) stepped up

sources.title: Wheat prices soar as Russia bans grain exports

title: Ukraine Invasion → Wheat and Food Shock
summary: Russia's invasion of Ukraine disrupted Black Sea grain exports from a major producing region and raised global food security concerns, driving food prices to record highs.
mechanism: Russia's invasion disrupted Ukrainian exports and shipping, removed a key exporter from global markets, and triggered food security concerns in import-dependent nations.
causal_chain: invasion disrupts Ukrainian agricultural production and exports
Black Sea shipping becomes hazardous
importers (Egypt, Turkey, MENA) face supply uncertainty
cereal prices reprice sharply higher
global food price index hits record in March 2022

forecast.note: Bullish wheat and grains; Ukraine + Russia together represent ~30% of global wheat exports

outcome.realized_note: FAO World Food Price Index hit record high in March 2022. MacroTrends wheat chart shows 2022 high at $12.94/bushel.
outcome.chart_note: MacroTrends wheat chart shows 2022 high at $12.94; FAO confirmed March 2022 peak shock
outcome.reversal_note: Black Sea Grain Initiative in July 2022 partially normalized shipping and prices eased

sources.title: War largely drives food prices to record levels

title: Indonesia Palm Oil Export Ban → Global Vegetable Oil Spike
summary: Indonesia, the world's largest palm oil producer, banned exports in April 2022 to address domestic cooking oil shortages, sending global vegetable oil prices sharply higher.
mechanism: Indonesia controls ~60% of global palm oil exports. An export ban immediately removes the majority of globally traded palm oil supply, forcing buyers to substitute with soy and rapeseed oil at a premium.
causal_chain: domestic cooking oil shortages create political pressure
Indonesia announces export ban effective April 28, 2022
global palm oil export availability falls sharply
substitute vegetable oils (soy, rapeseed) rally as buyers substitute
food inflation concerns deepen

forecast.note: Sharply bullish palm oil and substitute vegetable oils; Indonesia cannot sustain a prolonged ban without economic damage

outcome.realized_note: Palm oil prices spiked. Indonesia reversed the ban in late May 2022 after ~3 weeks due to domestic producer pressure.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — palm oil futures chart confirms spike around April-May 2022
outcome.priced_in_note: Coincided with already-elevated vegetable oil prices from Ukraine sunflower disruption
outcome.reversal_note: Price reversal was sharp when Indonesia announced the ban would be lifted

sources.title: Indonesia palm oil export ban coverage

title: India Rice Export Ban → Global Rice Price Spike
summary: India banned exports of non-basmati white rice in July 2023 amid domestic supply concerns, removing roughly 10 million tonnes of annual export capacity from global markets.
second_order_assets: food security in Asia, Africa, Caribbean
mechanism: India supplies ~40% of globally traded rice. An export ban removes a dominant supplier, forcing importers to compete for Thai, Vietnamese, Pakistani supply at a significant premium.
causal_chain: below-normal kharif sowing and El Niño uncertainty reduce domestic supply confidence
India bans non-basmati white rice exports
global rice buyers scramble for alternative origins
Thai and Vietnamese prices spike on demand surge
food security concerns emerge in import-dependent nations

forecast.note: Bullish global rice prices; India's 40% export share means alternatives are price-inelastic in the short term

outcome.realized_note: Global rice prices hit decade highs. Thai and Vietnamese benchmark prices rose ~15-20% in the weeks following the ban.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — Thai benchmark rice prices confirm spike in H2 2023

sources.title: India bans non-basmati white rice exports

title: India Wheat Export Ban → Tighter Global Supply
summary: India banned wheat exports in May 2022 after a heatwave cut its crop, compounding the Ukraine-driven global wheat shortage.
mechanism: India had stepped up as an alternative wheat supplier to replace Ukrainian volumes. The export ban reversed this, removing a key swing supplier from global trade at the worst time.
causal_chain: March 2022 heatwave cuts India wheat crop forecast
domestic supply concern prompts government intervention
India bans wheat exports effective May 14, 2022
importers reliant on Indian wheat must find alternatives
global wheat prices extend their 2022 rally

forecast.note: Incrementally bullish wheat given Ukraine war already dominant factor; India ban removes a swing supplier

outcome.realized_note: Wheat prices remained elevated. India ban was incremental to the Ukraine war shock already underway.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — wheat chart shows sustained 2022 elevated pricing
outcome.priced_in_note: Significant portion of Ukraine supply shock already priced in

title: U.S.–China Trade War Tariffs → Soybean Price Dislocation
summary: China retaliated against U.S. steel/aluminum tariffs by imposing 25% tariffs on U.S. soybeans, rerouting global soybean trade and crushing U.S. prices while boosting Brazilian premiums.
mechanism: China buys ~60% of globally traded soybeans. Targeting U.S. soybeans with tariffs diverts Chinese demand to Brazil, depressing U.S. basis and elevating Brazilian premiums. U.S. farmers face lower realized prices with no short-term alternative buyer.
causal_chain: U.S. imposes tariffs on Chinese goods
China retaliates with 25% tariff on U.S. soybeans effective July 6, 2018
Chinese crushers switch to Brazilian supply
U.S. soybean prices fall; Brazilian Paranaguá premiums spike
U.S. farmer income squeezed; storage and basis deteriorate

forecast.note: Bearish U.S. soybeans specifically; bullish Brazilian soybean basis; neutral to slightly bullish global meal prices

outcome.realized_note: U.S. soybean futures fell ~20% from pre-tariff levels. Brazilian premium surged. Trade disruption persisted until Phase 1 deal in 2020.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — CBOT soybean chart shows sharp 2018 selloff on tariff escalation
outcome.priced_in_note: Some tariff risk was priced before official announcement
outcome.reversal_note: Phase 1 trade deal January 2020 included Chinese soybean purchase commitments; prices partially recovered

sources.title: China's retaliatory tariffs on U.S. soybeans

title: OPEC Vienna Agreement → Oil Rally
summary: OPEC's first production cut deal in eight years, agreed at the Vienna meeting in November 2016, reversed a multi-year oversupply and lifted crude prices materially.
mechanism: After two years of deliberate overproduction strategy, OPEC agreed to cut ~1.2 million b/d. Non-OPEC (Russia) agreed to a separate ~0.6 million b/d cut. The coordinated supply withdrawal rebalanced the market faster than expected.
causal_chain: OPEC members agree to cut production by ~1.2 million b/d
Russia and non-OPEC agree to additional ~0.6 million b/d cut
market prices forward supply withdrawal
crude inventories begin drawing
Brent and WTI rally from ~$45 toward $55-60

forecast.note: Bullish crude; size and compliance of cut determines magnitude

outcome.realized_note: WTI rallied from ~$45 to ~$54 in the month following the deal. Compliance was better than historical OPEC averages.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — WTI/Brent chart confirms sharp November 2016 rally
outcome.priced_in_note: Some expectation priced in ahead of meeting on deal rumors
outcome.reversal_note: U.S. shale production response capped the rally over the following year

title: Saudi Arabia Refuses OPEC Output Cut → Oil Price Crash
summary: At the OPEC meeting in Vienna in November 2014, Saudi Arabia blocked a production cut despite prices already falling from $100+, triggering a crash from ~$80 to ~$45 over the following months.
mechanism: Saudi Arabia chose to defend market share over price, calculating that lower prices would squeeze U.S. shale and non-OPEC producers. The refusal to cut signaled a fundamental shift in OPEC strategy, removing the market's price floor.
causal_chain: oil prices already falling from $100 on rising U.S. shale output
OPEC November 2014 meeting: Saudi Arabia refuses to cut production
market interprets signal as removal of OPEC price floor
Brent falls from ~$80 to ~$45 over following months
U.S. shale producers begin hedging losses and cutting capex

forecast.note: Sharply bearish crude; removal of OPEC floor is a regime-change signal, not a temporary move

outcome.realized_note: Brent fell from ~$80 at meeting to below $50 by January 2015 and briefly below $30 in early 2016.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — Brent chart confirms multi-month crash into 2015-16
outcome.priced_in_note: Some decline already underway; Saudi decision accelerated and extended it
outcome.reversal_note: Prices recovered from early 2016 lows as shale capex cuts took effect and demand recovered

title: OPEC+ Historic 9.7 mb/d Cut → Oil Price Floor
summary: OPEC+ agreed to a record 9.7 million barrels per day production cut in April 2020 to stabilize markets after COVID demand collapse, though WTI still briefly went negative before cuts took effect.
mechanism: The largest production cut in OPEC history was designed to arrest the price collapse from COVID demand destruction. Though cuts took weeks to materialize, they provided a medium-term floor and enabled price recovery from sub-$20 levels.
causal_chain: COVID lockdowns collapse global oil demand by ~20-25 million b/d
WTI goes negative April 20, 2020 on storage crisis
OPEC+ agrees historic 9.7 mb/d cut April 12, effective May 1
market prices forward supply reduction
crude prices recover toward $40 by end of May 2020

forecast.note: Bullish but cautiously; demand recovery pace is the key uncertainty

outcome.realized_note: WTI recovered from negative territory to ~$40 by end of May and ~$45 by end of 2020. The OPEC+ framework held.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — WTI chart confirms recovery from April 2020 lows through year-end
outcome.priced_in_note: Demand recovery and vaccine timeline were significant concurrent factors

title: OPEC+ Surprise 2 mb/d Cut → Oil Rally
summary: OPEC+ announced a surprise 2 million barrel per day output cut in October 2022, defying Western pressure and reversing a brief price decline.
mechanism: With oil prices having declined from mid-2022 highs on global recession fears and demand destruction, Saudi Arabia led a larger-than-expected cut to defend prices above $90, creating geopolitical friction with the U.S. over energy security.
causal_chain: Brent declines from ~$120 toward ~$85-90 on demand fears
Saudi Arabia leads OPEC+ to agree 2 mb/d cut
market prices forward supply tightening
Brent rallies ~$5-10 in the days following announcement
geopolitical tensions rise between Riyadh and Washington

forecast.note: Bullish crude in the near term; medium-term dependent on demand trajectory

outcome.realized_note: Brent rallied ~$5-8 on the announcement. The price impact was partially offset by ongoing demand fears.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — Brent chart shows October 2022 bounce from ~$85
outcome.reversal_note: Prices drifted lower in late 2022 on China COVID restrictions and recession fears

title: Saudi Arabia Voluntary 1 mb/d Cut Extension → Oil Spike
summary: Saudi Arabia announced a voluntary 1 million b/d cut extension into August and beyond in July 2023, surprising markets and producing a sharp immediate oil rally.
mechanism: Saudi Arabia acted unilaterally beyond the OPEC+ baseline deal to tighten the physical market, signaling its willingness to defend a price target and extending the inventory draw.
causal_chain: Saudi Energy Minister announces voluntary 1 mb/d cut extension
market revises Q3 supply balance tighter
Brent spikes on announcement
inventory draws expected to accelerate
prices supported above $80

forecast.note: Bullish crude near-term; additional unilateral Saudi action is a strong commitment signal

outcome.realized_note: Brent rallied from ~$74 to ~$85-87 in the weeks following the announcement.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — Brent chart confirms July-September 2023 rally to ~$97

title: OPEC Oil Embargo → Quadrupling of Oil Prices
summary: Arab OPEC members embargoed oil exports to the U.S. and allies in retaliation for supporting Israel in the Yom Kippur War, causing the first global oil price shock.
mechanism: Arab OPEC members used oil as a geopolitical weapon. An embargo on target nations combined with production cuts quadrupled the global oil price in months, causing stagflationary recession.
causal_chain: Yom Kippur War begins October 6, 1973
Arab OPEC members announce embargo on U.S. and allies October 17
production cuts of ~5% per month imposed
oil price quadruples from ~$3 to ~$12/barrel by early 1974
global recession and inflation follow

forecast.note: Historically defining supply shock; no near-term substitutes available

outcome.realized_note: Crude oil price quadrupled from ~$3 to ~$12/barrel by early 1974. Caused global recession.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — historical oil price data confirms quadrupling in 1973-74
outcome.reversal_note: Embargo ended March 1974 but oil prices did not fully retrace

title: EU/U.S. Iran Oil Sanctions → Brent Premium
summary: The EU embargo on Iranian crude oil imports and tightened U.S. sanctions on Iran's central bank in early 2012 removed ~1 million b/d from the global market and lifted Brent.
mechanism: Iran was producing ~3.5 mb/d and exporting ~2.5 mb/d. Sanctions reducing exports by ~1 mb/d tightened the global balance and elevated the Brent geopolitical risk premium, amplified by Iranian threats to close the Strait of Hormuz.
causal_chain: EU announces ban on Iranian crude imports effective July 2012
U.S. sanctions target Iran's central bank, making financial transactions difficult
Iranian crude exports fall by ~1 mb/d over H1 2012
Brent trades above WTI with expanded premium
Hormuz closure threats add risk premium

forecast.note: Bullish Brent; Hormuz risk premium and reduced supply support price

outcome.realized_note: Brent traded above $120/barrel in the spring of 2012. Iranian exports fell materially.
outcome.chart_note: Provisional — Brent chart confirms elevated Q1-Q2 2012 pricing
outcome.priced_in_note: Saudi Arabia signaled willingness to offset Iranian volumes, which capped the upside

sources.title: EU sanctions on Iran oil imports
